summary: GDPR Chapter V (Arts. 44-50) establishes a hierarchical transfer regime for personal data to third countries. Art. 44 sets the 'non-undermining' principle: EU protection must travel with the data. Art. 45 (adequacy decisions) is the gold-standard route, requiring Commission assessment of rule of law, independent DPA, and effective data subject rights, with periodic 4-year reviews. Art. 46 permits transfers under appropriate safeguards — SCCs (Commission Implementing Decision 2021/914), BCRs (Art. 47, requiring legally binding group-wide rules approved via consistency mechanism), codes of conduct, and certification. Art. 48 blocks compliance with third-country judicial/administrative orders absent an MLAT. Art. 49 allows narrow derogations (explicit informed consent, contract necessity, public interest, legal claims, vital interests) that must be interpreted restrictively and cannot become the rule. Schrems II (CJEU C-311/18, 2020-07-16) imposed the 'essentially equivalent' protection standard, invalidated Privacy Shield for failing Charter Arts. 7/8/47 (US FISA 702 surveillance, no effective redress for non-citizens, Ombudsperson lacked independence), preserved SCCs conditionally, and crystallised the supervisory authority's duty to suspend non-compliant transfers. EDPB Recommendations 01/2020 (v2.0, 2021-06-18) operationalised Schrems II with a 6-step Transfer Impact Assessment methodology and supplementary-measures framework grounded in Art. 5(2) accountability. EDPB Guidelines 05/2021 clarify the Art. 3 / Chapter V interplay; Guidelines 2/2018 confirm Art. 49's restrictive interpretation. From the EU side, Korea received an adequacy decision on 2021-12-17 (Commission Implementing Decision (EU) 2022/254) covering the commercial sector based on PIPA plus PIPC Notification 2021-5, making Korea the first Asian country with broad commercial adequacy — EDPB Opinion 32/2021 had provided conditional support.

key_findings.finding: GDPR Chapter V imposes a strict hierarchy: Art. 45 adequacy decisions (preferred) > Art. 46 appropriate safeguards (SCCs, BCRs, codes, certifications) > Art. 49 derogations (last resort, restrictively interpreted). Art. 44 'non-undermining' clause is the overarching interpretive principle — EU protection level must be preserved wherever data travels.

Under Art. 49(1)(a), explicit consent is a derogation (not the primary legal basis for transfers). It requires prior information about the specific risks arising from absence of adequacy and safeguards. EDPB Guidelines 2/2018 interpret this narrowly: consent must be explicit, specific, informed, and cannot support repetitive/mass transfers. Consent-based transfers cannot 'become the rule'.

Art. 5(2) accountability places burden of proof on the data exporter (controller/processor). Post-Schrems II, exporters must perform case-by-case Transfer Impact Assessments (TIAs) per EDPB Recommendations 01/2020's 6-step methodology: map transfers, verify transfer tool, assess third-country law/practice, identify supplementary measures (technical/contractual/organisational), execute formal steps, re-evaluate. BCRs (Art. 47) require extensive documentation and group-wide liability acceptance.

National DPAs are the primary enforcers, with Art. 58 corrective powers. GDPR Art. 83(5)(c) imposes administrative fines up to EUR 20 million or 4% of global annual turnover for Chapter V violations — the highest fine tier. Schrems II (§§121, 135, 146) confirmed DPAs' independent duty to act on Chapter V violations regardless of Commission adequacy determinations. EDPB consistency mechanism (Art. 63) ensures cross-border coordination.

Schrems II crystallised a dual suspension duty: (1) the exporter must self-suspend transfers if third-country law/practice prevents effective SCC compliance; (2) the competent supervisory authority must suspend or prohibit transfers when safeguards cannot be observed. Art. 45(5) permits the Commission to repeal, amend, or suspend adequacy decisions upon changed circumstances; such action is without prejudice to Art. 46-49 transfers (Art. 45(7)). Art. 48 further bars compliance with third-country government orders absent an MLAT or international agreement.

EU granted Korea adequacy on 2021-12-17 (Commission Implementing Decision (EU) 2022/254), making Korea the first Asian country with broad commercial-sector adequacy. Decision is based on PIPA plus PIPC Notification 2021-5 (which provides additional safeguards for EU data subjects, notably on onward transfers and public authority access). EDPB Opinion 32/2021 gave conditional support. The decision is subject to 4-year periodic review (Art. 45(3)).
